title: Quiz: Resource Interconversion
description: Test your understanding of how different quantum resources can be converted and traded

questions.question: Coherence can be converted to entanglement by:

options.text: Using CNOT with a coherent control qubit and incoherent target

questions.explanation: A CNOT gate with a coherent state as control and |0⟩ as target converts the coherence to entanglement. For example, CNOT(|+⟩|0⟩) = (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2, creating a Bell state from coherence.

questions.question: A catalyst in a resource theory is:

options.text: A state that is consumed during the transformation
A state that enables a transformation while remaining unchanged
A free state that can be prepared at no cost

questions.explanation: A catalyst enables transformations that would otherwise be impossible, while remaining unchanged: ρ ⊗ σ → ρ' ⊗ σ. The catalyst σ participates in the transformation but is not consumed.

questions.question: In quantum teleportation, which resources are exchanged?
questions.explanation: Quantum teleportation consumes 1 ebit of entanglement and 2 classical bits to transmit 1 qubit. This is the fundamental resource trade-off: entanglement + classical communication = quantum communication.

questions.question: The relationship between coherence in the energy basis and work is:

options.text: Coherence destroys the ability to extract work
Coherence provides an additional contribution to free energy

questions.explanation: Coherence in the energy basis contributes to the free energy: F(ρ) = F_classical + T·C_rel(ρ). This coherent contribution can be accessed with appropriate (non-thermal) protocols.

questions.question: Magic states can be converted to entanglement, but entanglement alone cannot create magic.
questions.explanation: True. Magic states enable universal quantum computation and can create any state including entangled states. However, entanglement alone (with Clifford operations) cannot create magic - this is the essence of the Gottesman-Knill theorem.

questions.question: Dense coding demonstrates that:

options.text: Entanglement can be created from classical bits
1 ebit + 1 qubit transmission can send 2 classical bits
Quantum bits cost twice as much as classical bits

questions.explanation: Superdense coding shows that sharing 1 ebit and transmitting 1 qubit allows communication of 2 classical bits. This is the reverse of teleportation: using entanglement to boost classical capacity.

questions.question: In the asymptotic limit, the exchange rate between coherence and entanglement is:

options.text: Determined by the relative entropy of coherence

questions.explanation: In the asymptotic limit, n copies of a coherent state can be converted to m ≈ n·C_rel(ρ) ebits. The relative entropy of coherence determines the asymptotic exchange rate.

questions.question: Which statements about resource interconversion are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Some transformations impossible without catalyst become possible with one
Resource interconversion is always perfectly efficient
Different resource theories share structural similarities
Entanglement embezzlement can approximately create any state with large catalyst

questions.explanation: (a) Correct: Catalysis enables otherwise impossible transformations. (b) Incorrect: Conversion often involves loss (irreversibility). (c) Correct: All resource theories have similar axiomatic structure. (d) Correct: With sufficiently large embezzling states, any transformation is approximately possible.
